GateDocument.TextWithNodes: "Alessandro Michele reimagined the Gucci Sydney flagship — and it's just opened", "Alessandro Michele's ethos is captured in the Italian-house's newly reopened Sydney flagship.", "If Gucci creative director Alessandro Michele's creative forays have taught us anything, it's that fashion is a 360-degree, multi-faceted, multi-disciplinary experience. It was a message driven home in his epic collection showing in July, where models, designers from his studio, photographers, and the mammoth team effort it takes to put together a campaign were on show shooting the collection in a live 12-hour stream in lieu of a runway show back in July. And it is this free-thinking and forward-facing ethos that is captured in the Italian-house's brand new, just-unveiled Gucci Sydney flagship, where Michele has re-imagined the space which officially opened last week and where the same resort collection was previewed (available later this month), as part of the celebration of the sprawling 11,702 square foot store's re-opening. Almost a year-long considered renovation has given birth to a boutique of grand proportions — the store is bigger than it was pre-renewal — and intrinsically Gucci sensibility; read rich detail, opulent rugs, velvet armchairs with contrasting elements that speak to the contemporary eclecticism of Michele's overall vision. This means a hand-painted herringbone wooden floor sits alongside industrial elements like polished metal and a clever, not cluttered, use of space. This extends, in a very Gucci full circle, to the wares. A treasure trove of accessories — from shoes, watches, jewellery, eyewear to the house's iconic bags (welcome, the Jackie to the line-up of forever favourites) — join graphic 70s wallpaper prints and eye-popping geometrics in the clothes that would woo a young Marisa Berenson. It is all part of the collector's sensibility that Michele has been imprinted upon the house DNA and was shown at the Palazzo Sacchetti in Rome, a building dating back to the Renaissance for the resort outing. Our pick of pieces from the line-up, when it drops in store, include trailing silk pussy bows, head scarves in shades of peony and emerald, kaleidoscopic intarsia knits and maxi-dresses with a bouquet of florals, or a particularly striking paisley two-piece skirt and one-shoulder top. Contrasting elements, a Michele hallmark, appear in the clean lines of metallic and monogrammed-suiting and more structured A-line skirts and separates set against the louche maxi-dresses and flowing bishop sleeves, chain belts and gilded brooches. Dubbed 'epilogue', the name is a nod to it being his last-ever resort collection for the house, boosting its collectible-credentials. And because this is Gucci, no store opening would be complete without an added dose of pure creativity. Here it comes in the form of a collaboration with local art collective O.Z.O. of which local artists Edward Woodley and Max Berry belong, the former making a custom sculpture replete with mirrored panels to draw the viewer around it, the latter projecting wistful vision of Gucci's collections across the entirety of a wall on the ground floor. Woodley's work filled with saturated brights and featuring the iconic monogram took pride of place on the store's facade for the grand opening. Our tip? Get in before the mini Jackie in snakeskin is snapped up, or invest in any one of Gucci's line-up of classic Beloved bags, in timeless styles and proportions — the GG Marmont, Horsebit 1955 or Dionysus. How else to mark the occasion?"
